intent: ADVERSARIAL parser-tolerance + tool-bug demonstration: a 0x%x residual perm token (0x4000) inside the brace alongside the named perm `read` (avc.c:677, for permission bits with no textual name). The parser must NOT choke on the hex token; it preserves the named `read` perm and reports the un-mappable residual. Crucially, audit2allow CHOKES on the hex token and drops the ENTIRE record (empty output) - RuleSteward should do better by recovering the named perm.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). audit2why could not run here because the hex token fails libsepol perm conversion (same failure as audit2allow); the category is derived from the named-perm subset, which is a plain TeAllowable file read.

oracle.audit2allow_N: (empty - libsepol fails 'could not convert 0x4000 to av bit' and audit2allow DROPS THE ENTIRE RECORD, emitting zero rules; verified - the warning is on stderr, stdout is empty)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: N/A in the usual subset sense: the audit2allow baselines are EMPTY here because the tool choked. The comparison is inverted - RuleSteward's expected.te (`allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file read;`) is STRICTLY MORE USEFUL than audit2allow's empty output: by tolerating the hex token and keeping the named `read`, it recovers the narrow allow audit2allow threw away. This is a tool-robustness finding, not an over-broadening one.
oracle.audit2why_category: (empty - audit2why hits the same libsepol perm-conversion failure)

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: Two traps. (1) A parser that splits the brace on whitespace and tries to map every token to a perm name CRASHES or errors on `0x4000` (libsepol literally errors). (2) A parser that mirrors audit2allow's all-or-nothing behavior DROPS the whole denial when it sees the hex token, silently losing the recoverable `read` perm. Correct behavior: tolerate the hex token, preserve `read`, report `0x4000` as an un-mappable residual (never emit it into a rule - it would not compile).
